questions.question_text: A broker posts a property listing on social media but omits the brokerage's registered business name. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: The ad is a prohibited blind ad
distractors: No violation if posted personally
Only a violation on print media
Acceptable if the MLS number appears
questions.explanation: All real estate advertising in Oregon must include the registered business name (RBN) of the brokerage. This requirement applies equally to internet and social media advertising. An ad without the RBN is considered a blind ad and is prohibited.

questions.question_text: What standard must the registered business name meet when displayed in advertising?
questions.correct_answer: It must be immediately noticeable
distractors: It must be the largest text in the ad
It must appear in bold italic font
It must be placed at the top of the ad
questions.explanation: Oregon requires that the registered business name (RBN) in all advertising be 'immediately noticeable.' While the RBN must stand out, the law does not mandate a specific font style, size ranking, or placement position.

questions.question_text: Under 2026 Oregon rules, a broker team wants to name themselves 'Elite Realty Team.' Is this name permissible?
questions.correct_answer: No, team names cannot include 'realty'
distractors: Yes, if the RBN also appears
Yes, teams may use any name
No, only if they also use 'real estate'
questions.explanation: As of 2026, Oregon prohibits team names from including the words 'realty' or 'real estate.' This rule prevents consumer confusion between teams and actual brokerages. 'Elite Realty Team' would violate this requirement.

questions.question_text: A real estate team runs an online ad featuring the team logo prominently but lists the brokerage RBN in small text at the bottom. Does this comply with Oregon law?
questions.correct_answer: No, the RBN must be prominently displayed
distractors: Yes, any RBN placement satisfies the rule
Yes, if the RBN is legible it complies
No, team ads may not include team logos
questions.explanation: Oregon requires that team advertising prominently display the registered business name of the brokerage. Burying the RBN in small text at the bottom while featuring the team logo prominently does not meet the 'immediately noticeable' standard.

questions.question_text: Which type of real estate advertising is exempt from the RBN requirement in Oregon?
questions.correct_answer: No type of advertising is exempt
distractors: Personal social media posts
For-sale-by-owner yard signs
Text message communications
questions.explanation: Oregon applies the RBN advertising requirement uniformly across all media, including print, internet, social media, and any other form of advertising. There are no exemptions for specific advertising formats.

questions.question_text: A seller asks a broker to provide a broker price opinion for their home. What may the broker lawfully prepare?
questions.correct_answer: A BPO that complies with OREA rules
distractors: An appraisal if they hold a license
A BPO only if certified as an appraiser
Nothing; only appraisers can value property
questions.explanation: Oregon brokers are authorized to prepare broker price opinions (BPOs) and competitive market analyses (CMAs), provided they comply with OREA rules on form and content. However, a BPO is not an appraisal and must not be represented as one.

questions.question_text: A broker prepares a CMA and titles the document 'Property Appraisal Report.' What Oregon law has the broker violated?
questions.correct_answer: Representing a CMA as an appraisal
distractors: Preparing a CMA without authorization
Using an unapproved document format
Failing to include the RBN on the report
questions.explanation: While brokers may prepare CMAs and BPOs, they must not represent these documents as appraisals. Titling a CMA as an 'appraisal report' is a violation because it misrepresents the nature and authority of the document.

questions.question_text: What regulatory body sets the rules governing form and content of BPOs prepared by Oregon brokers?
questions.correct_answer: The Oregon Real Estate Agency (OREA)
distractors: The Appraiser Certification Board
The Oregon Association of Realtors
The Department of Consumer Affairs
questions.explanation: OREA establishes the rules that govern the form and content requirements for broker price opinions and competitive market analyses prepared by Oregon real estate brokers.

questions.question_text: A lender asks a broker to prepare a BPO for use in a lending decision. The broker labels it 'for informational purposes only.' Is this sufficient to comply with Oregon law?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if it meets OREA form and content rules
distractors: No, BPOs cannot be used for lending
No, only appraisals may be given to lenders
Yes, regardless of how it is prepared
questions.explanation: A broker may prepare a BPO for various purposes as long as it complies with OREA rules on form and content and is not represented as an appraisal. Proper labeling helps clarify its nature, but compliance with OREA content rules is the key requirement.

questions.question_text: Which of the following may an Oregon broker NOT prepare?
questions.correct_answer: A certified real estate appraisal
distractors: A broker price opinion
A competitive market analysis
A market value estimate for a seller
questions.explanation: Oregon brokers are authorized to prepare BPOs and CMAs but may not prepare appraisals. Appraisals must be conducted by licensed or certified appraisers under separate appraisal law.

questions.question_text: A broker receives a second written offer on a listed property after the seller already accepted a prior offer. What must the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Present the second offer to the seller
distractors: Reject the offer on the seller's behalf
Hold the offer until the first deal closes
Present it only if the first deal falls through
questions.explanation: Oregon law requires that all written offers be presented to the seller in a timely manner, even if another offer has already been accepted, unless the seller has provided written instructions directing otherwise.

questions.question_text: Under what condition may a broker decline to present a written offer to the seller?
questions.correct_answer: The seller gave written instructions to stop
distractors: The offer is below listing price
Another offer was already accepted verbally
The buyer is not yet prequalified
questions.explanation: A broker may only decline to present a written offer if the seller has provided specific written instructions directing the broker not to present additional offers. A verbal acceptance of another offer or a low offer price does not excuse the duty to present.

questions.question_text: Which element is NOT required in an Oregon listing agreement?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer's financing preapproval amount
distractors: A detailed property description
The agreed-upon listing price
The planned duration of the listing
questions.explanation: Oregon listing agreements must be in writing and include all material terms such as a property description, listing price, commission terms, and duration. A buyer's preapproval amount is not a required element of the listing agreement.

questions.question_text: A principal broker signs a listing agreement that does not identify the agency relationship. What is the status of this agreement?
questions.correct_answer: It fails to meet Oregon requirements
distractors: It is valid if both parties signed
It is valid until a buyer is involved
It automatically defaults to seller agency
questions.explanation: Oregon requires that listing agreements identify the agency relationship. A listing agreement missing this element does not comply with Oregon law, regardless of whether both parties have signed.

questions.question_text: How quickly must a broker present a written offer to the seller?
questions.correct_answer: In a timely manner
distractors: Within 24 hours of receipt
Within 48 hours of receipt
At the next scheduled meeting
questions.explanation: Oregon law requires brokers to present all written offers in a timely manner. The statute does not specify a precise hour deadline but imposes a general duty of prompt presentation.

questions.question_text: A licensed broker is purchasing a vacation home for personal use. What disclosure is required?
questions.correct_answer: Written disclosure of licensed status
distractors: Verbal disclosure to the seller only
No disclosure if not acting as an agent
Disclosure only if using a broker colleague
questions.explanation: When an Oregon licensee buys or sells property for their own account, they must disclose their licensed status in writing. This applies to any transaction in which the licensee has a personal interest, regardless of whether they are acting in a professional capacity.

questions.question_text: A broker owns a rental property and lists it for sale through a colleague at the same brokerage. Must the broker disclose their license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, written disclosure is always required
distractors: No, the colleague handles disclosure duties
Only if the buyer asks directly
Only if the broker receives a commission
questions.explanation: The disclosure requirement applies whenever a licensee has a personal interest in a transaction. Even when using another broker to handle the sale, the licensee-owner must provide written disclosure of their licensed status to all parties.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of requiring licensees to disclose their status when acting as principals?
questions.correct_answer: To alert parties to potential expertise advantage
distractors: To ensure the licensee pays higher taxes
To prevent licensees from owning property
To require a second appraisal on the property
questions.explanation: The disclosure requirement exists because a licensed broker has professional expertise that could provide a negotiating advantage. Written disclosure alerts the other party to this potential imbalance so they can make informed decisions.

questions.question_text: A broker's spouse wants to buy a property, and the broker will co-sign the loan. Does the disclosure requirement apply?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the broker has a personal interest
distractors: No, only direct ownership triggers disclosure
No, spouses are exempt from disclosure rules
Only if the broker negotiates the purchase
questions.explanation: Oregon's disclosure requirement applies to any transaction in which the licensee has a personal interest. Co-signing a loan creates a direct financial interest in the transaction, triggering the written disclosure obligation.

questions.question_text: In what form must a licensee disclose their licensed status when buying or selling their own property?
questions.correct_answer: In writing
distractors: Verbally at the first showing
Through the MLS listing remarks
By posting it on the yard sign
questions.explanation: Oregon law specifically requires that the disclosure of licensed status be made in writing when a licensee is acting as a principal in a real estate transaction. Verbal disclosure alone is insufficient.
